facts.text: DUMBO stands for “Down Under the Manhattan Bridge Overpass,” a nickname coined in the 1970s when warehouses still lined the waterfront.
The neighborhood began as a ferry landing, later filled with 19th‑century warehouses, Belgian block streets, and the looming Manhattan Bridge.
Tracks of the Jay Street Connecting Railroad ran along Dumbo’s waterfront from 1904 until 1957, and remnants still trace the streets.
In December 2000, chocolatier Jacques Torres opened a factory in Dumbo, turning a former warehouse into a sweet‑scented landmark.
The DUMBO Archway, a steel frame over the river, hosts film shoots, art shows, and even World Cup watch parties.
Ice cream at the Factory and a stroll into Brooklyn Bridge Park let visitors taste history while enjoying river views.
